symbols: Christ rising from the tomb — the Man of Sorrows, dead yet standing, between death and resurrection
visible wound in side — the mark of the lance that pierced the Crucified
cross visible behind — the instrument of death now a sign of victory
gold roundel — divine glory surrounding the suffering Christ
half-length emerging from sarcophagus — the threshold between death and life
description: A small devotional painting in a triangular gold-leaf spandrel, likely from the pinnacle of an altarpiece. Within a dark blue circular roundel, Christ is depicted half-length, rising from a pink sarcophagus. His eyes are closed, his head slightly bowed, wearing a halo with a cross. His bare torso shows the wound in his side. The cross rises behind him. His hands rest on the edge of the sarcophagus, displaying the nail wounds. The gold-leaf triangular field surrounding the roundel is decorated with a delicate punched border pattern.
theological_description: The Imago Pietatis (Image of Pity) is one of the most theologically condensed images in Christian art. It depicts Christ neither as simply dead on the cross nor fully risen — but in an impossible liminal state that transcends historical time. He stands upright yet bears the wounds; His eyes are closed in death yet He is displayed for veneration. This is the Eucharistic Christ — the one who is perpetually offered in the sacrifice of the Mass, always dying yet always alive. The image draws from Isaiah's Suffering Servant: 'He was despised and rejected by men, a man of sorrows and acquainted with grief' (ISA.53.3). The visible wound in the side recalls John's account of the soldier's lance: 'one of the soldiers pierced his side, and at once there came out blood and water' (JHN.19.34) — the blood and water understood sacramentally as Eucharist and Baptism. The gold-leaf setting affirms that this suffering figure is simultaneously the Lord of Glory.

scripture_refs.reason: The Man of Sorrows, acquainted with grief, wounded for our transgressions
The pierced side from which blood and water flowed — the visible wound in the image
The Lamb standing as though slain — alive yet bearing the marks of death
Proclaiming the Lord's death until He comes — the Eucharistic mystery the Imago Pietatis represents
